items.myth: The Hessian is always invertible at a minimum.
items.reality: Flat or degenerate directions give zero eigenvalues—a positive semidefinite but singular Hessian. Newton's method then needs damping or pseudo-inverses.
items.example: Loss L(w₁,w₂)=w₁² has minimum at w₁=0 but H=[[2,0],[0,0]] is singular in w₂; infinitely many equivalent minima along the flat direction.

items.myth: Jacobian and gradient are unrelated objects.
items.reality: For scalar f, the gradient is the Jacobian arranged as a column (or the transpose of the 1×n Jacobian row). They store the same first partial derivatives.
items.example: For f(x,y)=x²+y, Jacobian row is [2x, 1] and ∇f = (2x, 1)ᵀ at any point.

items.myth: If the gradient is zero, the Hessian tells you nothing useful.
items.reality: At critical points where ∇f=0, the Hessian is the leading term in the Taylor expansion and determines whether the point is a min, max, or saddle.
items.example: f(x,y)=x²−y² has ∇f=0 at origin; H with eigenvalues +2 and −2 confirms a saddle, not a minimum.

items.myth: Second-order methods always beat gradient descent for deep learning.
items.reality: Full Newton steps are too expensive and noisy stochastic Hessians are hard to estimate; Adam/SGD dominate because first-order methods scale and tolerate mini-batch noise.
items.example: A 7B-parameter model would need ~49 trillion Hessian entries—impossible to store; L-BFGS is used only in small subspaces or fine-tuning.
